children.author: bonesss
children.textRawHtml:
So “landlord” and “commercial landlord living entirely off of passive income” are worlds apart.

Buying a fixer-upper outside of town with high-school and early 20s grinding, renting out 3+ rooms to cover the mortgage for painful years, working 80 hour weeks, refinancing against that first house into another under-maintained property where you live in half while upgrading the other, ending up with a rental duplex and drastically reduced living cost, is viable by 30. Maximizing youth savings, first house programs, and primary residence rules create less punitive economics.

It sucks and will let one learn why landlord is a pain in the ass job, and relies on sweat equity and modest lifestyle, wanting to commit to real estate, and non-ideal properties. Trade school or skipping college for early income and low debt make the numbers crunch easier.

Investing consistently into the market in your 20s probably out performs it by 65, and a young bankers lifestyle is a joy of its own, but: owning property young is achievable for electricians, security guards, and janitors.
children.textZhHtml:
所以 ”房东“ 和 ”商业房东完全靠被动收入生活” 是天壤之别。

20岁出头的高中生在城外买一套待修的房子，出租3个以上的房间来偿还抵押贷款，度过痛苦的岁月，每周工作80小时，用第一套房子再融资到另一套维护不善的房产，你住在一半，同时升级另一套房子，最终得到一套出租复式公寓，生活成本大幅降低，这在30岁之前是可行的。最大限度地提高年轻人的储蓄、第一套房子计划和主要居住规则可以减少惩罚性经济。

它很糟糕，会让人们了解到为什么房东是一份令人痛苦的工作，并依赖于汗水资产和朴素的生活方式，想要致力于房地产和非理想的房产。为了早期收入和低债务而进入贸易学校或跳过大学会让数字变得更容易处理。

20多岁时持续投资市场可能比65岁时的表现要好，年轻的银行家生活方式本身就是一种乐趣，但是：年轻时拥有房产对于电工、保安和清洁工来说是可以实现的。

children.author: b40d-48b2-979e
children.textRawHtml:
owning property young is achievable for electricians, security guards, and janitors.
The comment wasn't about buying your first house, it was "owning several properties by the time you're 30" which a janitor of all things absolutely cannot do.
children.textZhHtml:
owning property young is achievable for electricians, security guards, and janitors.
该评论不是关于购买你的第一套房子，而是“在你 30 岁的时候拥有几处房产” 看门人 凡事绝对不能做。

children.author: bonesss
children.textRawHtml:
Read my comment again, and think more about what was said. I addressed multiple properties before 30 as a landlord, not first time buying as an employee. Because you are not accounting for cash flow & appreciation over time you are flat wrong.

A non-landlord janitor saddled with student loans losing 30%+ of income to housing versus a landlord whose housing is covered that works as a janitor from the get-go have wildly different leverage opportunities and savings potential at 20 and 5 years down the road. Committing to property ownership instead of school and maximally exploiting living at home gets the down payment, committing to property improvement instead of lifestyle is what gears the investment.

That massive monthly rental savings snowballs into a down payment in the 5 year picture, the first assets improvements and cash flow support lending for the subsequent property purchase, upgrades to which support refinancing and correcting cash flow in the original property. Backed by those assets and cash flow: multi-tenant properties, incorporation, or more aggressive flipping are straight shots backed by the appreciating assets and ongoing work income.

Janitors can clean on the side, work in corporate chains, work in secure facilities, make overtime, juggle multiple jobs, or snowball their hustle into a cleaning company. A landlord-janitor can be creating a crew of live-together like-minded grinders and be building business wealth in parallel to their rental business in a synergistic loop.

It is very possible, I know several people who done it, and know of numerous successful businesses structured around the same. A group of immigrants in a house with a cleaning van out front can be a respectable business. Five such houses could be an early retirement.

The conceptual breakdown tends to be in willingness to sacrifice, and do hard unglamorous work.

“Janitors of all things” can be smart entrepreneurs who grow wealth, just like how programmers, of all things, can misunderstand basic financial calculus.
children.textZhHtml:
再次阅读我的评论，并更多地思考我所说的内容。我在30岁之前作为房东处理过多处房产，而不是第一次作为雇员购买。因为你没有考虑现金流和随着时间的推移升值，所以你完全错了。

一个背负学生贷款的非房东看门人，他的收入会损失 30% 以上的住房收入，而与一个拥有住房保障但从一开始就担任看门人的房东相比，在未来 20 年和 5 年的杠杆机会和储蓄潜力截然不同。致力于拥有财产而不是学校，并最大限度地利用在家生活可以获得首付，致力于改善财产而不是生活方式才是投资的动力。

每月节省的大量租金会在 5 年内滚雪球般转化为首付，首次资产改善和现金流支持为后续购买房产提供贷款，升级则支持再融资并纠正原有房产的现金流。在这些资产和现金流的支持下：多租户房产、公司合并或更激进的翻转都是在资产升值和持续工作收入的支持下直接进行的。

看门人 可以兼职清洁、在连锁企业工作、在安全设施中工作、加班、兼顾多项工作，或者滚雪球般地加入清洁公司。房东兼看门人可以组建一群志同道合的磨工，在开展租赁业务的同时创造商业财富，形成协同循环。

这很有可能，我认识几个做过这件事的人，也知道许多成功的企业都是围绕同样的事情构建的。一群移民住在一所房子里，前面有一辆清洁车，这可能是一件值得尊敬的事情。五套这样的房子就可以提前退休了。

观念的崩溃往往在于愿意牺牲、做艰苦、乏味的工作。

“万物看门人”可以是增加财富的聪明企业家，就像程序员可能会误解基本的金融计算一样。

children.author: scottyah
children.textRawHtml: Live in a poor place. There are plenty of cheap houses, but you do have to leave expensive areas (shocking, I know).
children.textZhHtml: 住在穷地方。有很多便宜的房子，但你必须离开昂贵的地区（我知道这很令人震惊）。

children.author: cool_dude85
children.textRawHtml: So, are the landlords wealthy or not wealthy? I don't get it.
children.textZhHtml: 那么，楼主到底是有钱还是没钱呢？我不明白。

children.author: jagged-chisel
children.textRawHtml: The annoying/sad/infuriating thing is the ultra wealthy don’t have “income.” Technically, according to IRS rules, much of what they experience (housing, food, etc) should be classified as income. But their lawyers and accountants help them keep that looking quite low.
children.textZhHtml: 令人烦恼/悲伤/愤怒的是超级富豪没有“收入”。从技术上讲，根据国税局的规定，他们的大部分经历（住房、食物等）都应归类为收入。但他们的律师和会计师帮助他们保持较低的水平。

children.author: Schiendelman
children.textRawHtml: I used to think this - but when I talked to a tax lawyer friend and we walked through the steps they take, usually they're just deferring taxation that does end up getting paid by an entity eventually.
children.textZhHtml: 我曾经这么认为 - 但当我和一位税务律师朋友交谈并逐步了解他们采取的步骤时，通常他们只是 推迟 最终由实体支付的税收。

children.author: ck_one
children.textRawHtml: Capital gains tax is clearly lower than income tax. So why did you change your mind?
children.textZhHtml: 资本利得税明显低于所得税。那么你为什么改变主意呢？

children.author: nickff
children.textRawHtml:
Not the commenter you replied to, but one thing to note is that capital gains tax (at least in the context of investments in corporate equities) is applied after corporate taxes. Profits and reinvested earnings are taxed as profits, and they're two of the key components to valuing an equity.

As such, when comparing income tax and capital gains, you should add the impact of corporate taxes. Incidentally, corporate taxes are why many small business owners pay themselves wage income, rather than doing stock buybacks or dividends.
children.textZhHtml:
不是您回复的评论者，但需要注意的一件事是，资本利得税（至少在公司股权投资的背景下）是在公司税之后征收的。利润和再投资收益作为利润征税，它们是股权估值的两个关键组成部分。

因此，在比较所得税和资本利得时，您应该考虑公司税的影响。顺便说一句，公司税是许多小企业主向自己支付工资收入，而不是进行股票回购或股息的原因。

children.author: topgrain2
children.textRawHtml:
> Incidentally, corporate taxes are why many small business owners pay themselves wage income, rather than doing stock buybacks or dividends.

You've been sold some BS. Usually this is because you're required to take a "reasonable" wage for your role in a company. Otherwise I guarantee you every independent contractor out there (among others) would be operating in a way that made 100% of their income business profit, rather than wages, as it has enormous tax advantages. Approximately everybody tries to find out the least they can take as wage income without pissing off the IRS, and sets their "wage" to whatever that is.
children.textZhHtml:
> 顺便说一句，公司税是许多小企业主向自己支付工资收入，而不是进行股票回购或股息的原因。

你被卖了一些废话。通常这是因为你 必需的 为您在公司中的角色获取“合理”的工资。否则，我向您保证，每个独立承包商（以及其他承包商）都会以 100% 的收入商业利润而不是工资的方式运营，因为它具有巨大的税收优势。几乎每个人都试图找出 至少 他们可以在不惹恼国税局的情况下将其视为工资收入，并将他们的“工资”设定为任何数字。

children.author: nickff
children.textRawHtml: Many locales have laws that do not allow remuneration above the 'reasonable wage', to prevent tax circumvention by having employers spread wage payments across multiple family members of employees, but I am not familiar with any jurisdiction with a minimum reasonable wage law or regulation. Could you please link some source for the claim that business owners are required to accept a 'reasonable wage'?
children.textZhHtml: 许多地区的法律不允许薪酬高于“合理工资”，以防止雇主将工资分摊给雇员的多名家庭成员来规避税收，但我不熟悉任何有最低合理工资法律或法规的司法管辖区。您能否链接一些有关企业主必须接受“合理工资”的说法的来源？

children.author: topgrain2
children.textRawHtml: https://www.irs.gov/businesses/small-businesses-self-employe...
children.textZhHtml: https://www.irs.gov/businesses/small-businesses-self-employe...

children.author: nickff
children.textRawHtml:
Yes, that is intended to disallow officers from avoiding taxes by being compensated via ‘loans’ and other means. I can’t find any case where dividends or buybacks were found to be violations of that rule. https://cpataxteam.com/blog/s-corp-owners-are-you-paying-you...

Do the math for yourself. Paying corporate taxes on profits, then dividend taxes on what gets paid out is not a savings versus paying income+payroll tax (which comes from money that is treated as an expense at the corporate level).
children.textZhHtml:
是的，这是为了禁止官员通过“贷款”和其他方式获得补偿来避税。我找不到任何股息或回购被发现违反该规则的案例。 https://cpataxteam.com/blog/s-corp-owners-are-you-paying-you...

自己算算。缴纳公司利润税，然后对所支付的股息税，与缴纳所得税+工资税（来自在公司层面被视为费用的资金）相比，并不是一种储蓄。

children.author: topgrain2
children.textRawHtml: There are more taxes than federal income and corporate taxes. State taxes and especially the ~15% of wages that go to FICA also matter (the topic was small businesses, so presumably the owner's not making so much more than the FICA limit that this becomes negligible).
children.textZhHtml: 税收比联邦所得税和公司税还要多。州税，尤其是 FICA 的约 15% 的工资也很重要（主题是 小的 企业，因此大概所有者的收入不会超过 FICA 限额，因此这一点可以忽略不计）。

children.author: tonyedgecombe
children.textRawHtml: I've often wondered why we don't abolish corporation tax and instead tax capital gains and dividends like normal income.
children.textZhHtml: 我经常想知道为什么我们不废除公司税，而是像正常收入一样对资本利得和股息征税。

children.author: nickff
children.textRawHtml: This would be my personal preference, as I believe that voters often overlook the impact of corporate taxes, and there are just too many (different) taxes.
children.textZhHtml: 这是我个人的偏好，因为我认为选民经常忽视公司税的影响，而且（不同的）税收太多了。

children.author: daedrdev
children.textRawHtml: Because capital gains taxes really discourage selling which gums up the economy
children.textZhHtml: 因为资本利得税确实会抑制销售，从而阻碍经济发展

children.author: triceratops
children.textRawHtml: Wouldn't prices of assets just rise to compensate? People still need liquidity.
children.textZhHtml: 难道资产价格不会上涨来补偿吗？人们仍然需要流动性。

children.author: nickff
children.textRawHtml: From what I understand, this lack of liquidity is not an issue in ‘financial markets’, but can be a problem for other assets such as housing.
children.textZhHtml: 据我了解，流动性缺乏并不是“金融市场”的问题，但可能是住房等其他资产的问题。

children.author: saghm
children.textRawHtml:
If the income was earned through dividends, maybe this would be a reasonable argument. Most of the time stock just gets bought and sold by investors rather than the company itself though, so it's not clear why corporate tax would have anything to do with this.

Sure, the stock price should somehow be tied to the actual value of the company, but for a while now it's been mostly indistinguishable from a Ponzi scheme other than a few companies that do sometimes decide to buy back some stock, which makes it slightly less sketchy but if the value is from the company buying it back, it's a lot closer to debt or a bond, which is not at all how anyone treats it.
children.textZhHtml:
如果收入是通过股息赚取的，也许这是一个合理的论点。大多数情况下，股票只是由投资者而非公司本身买卖，因此尚不清楚为什么公司税与此有任何关系。

当然，还有股价 应该 以某种方式与公司的实际价值挂钩，但一段时间以来，它与庞氏骗局几乎没有区别，除了少数公司有时会决定回购一些股票，这使得它与庞氏骗局几乎没有区别。 轻微地 不太粗略，但如果价值来自公司回购，那么它更接近债务或债券，而这根本不是任何人对待它的方式。

children.author: nickff
children.textRawHtml: I agree that in a bull market, many corporations are not purchased and sold at book value. That said, we are on the largest bull-run in history, so we shouldn’t treat this as the norm, and base all our long-term decisions on the current situation.
children.textZhHtml: 我同意，在牛市中，许多公司并不是按账面价值买卖的。也就是说，我们正处于历史上最大的牛市之中，因此我们不应该将此视为常态，而应根据当前情况做出所有长期决策。

children.author: saghm
children.textRawHtml: So if I'm understanding correctly, your argument is that it doesn't make sense to make people who make more money pay the same rate of taxes as regular people do because we should ignore the fact that they've been doing it for longer than ever before?
children.textZhHtml: 所以，如果我理解正确的话，你的论点是，让赚更多钱的人缴纳与普通人相同的税率是没有意义的，因为我们应该忽略他们这样做的时间比以前更长的事实？

children.author: nickff
children.textRawHtml: I’m saying that we shouldn’t base our tax policy on a bubble. The dot-com bubble was treated like a solution to the federal deficit, and it wasn’t.
children.textZhHtml: 我的意思是，我们不应该将税收政策建立在泡沫之上。互联网泡沫被视为解决联邦赤字的办法，但事实并非如此。

children.author: panzagl
children.textRawHtml: So if I buy and sell Pokeman cards I shouldn't have to pay any tax because WotC pays corporate taxes?
children.textZhHtml: 因此，如果我买卖 Pokeman 卡，我不需要缴纳任何税，因为 WotC 缴纳公司税？

children.author: nickff
children.textRawHtml: I am not saying that one party paying taxes means that no counter-party should. I am just saying that the impact of different structures should be accounted for.
children.textZhHtml: 我并不是说一方纳税就意味着任何交易对手都不应纳税。我只是说应该考虑不同结构的影响。

children.author: Schiendelman
children.textRawHtml: Their income would be capital gains regardless of whether they use these methods or not.
children.textZhHtml: 无论他们是否使用这些方法，他们的收入都将是资本收益。

children.author: toomuchtodo
children.textRawHtml:
If they donate the wealth to their own foundation to continue to hold close and control, it doesn't get taxed. If they borrow against the wealth at low interest rates until they die and the basis is stepped up ("buy, borrow, die"), it doesn't get taxed. Certainly, deferment is a component, but there are obvious examples of the very wealthy operating in a manner to avoid taxes entirely when they're able to (realizing the benefit of the wealth without having to realize a taxable event). Trust stacking is a recent fad as well, although I don't have enough data to say whether it is a material concern from a tax revenue perspective.

Silicon Valley Is Obsessed with 'Trust Stacking,' and the IRS Doesn't Like It - https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=48727963 - June 2026
children.textZhHtml:
如果他们将财富捐赠给自己的基金会以继续持有和控制，则不会被征税。如果他们以低利率抵押财富直至去世，并且基础增加（“买、借、死”），则不会被征税。当然，延期是一个组成部分，但有明显的例子表明，非常富有的人在有能力的情况下以完全避税的方式经营（实现财富的利益，而不必实现应税事件）。信任叠加也是最近的一种时尚，尽管我没有足够的数据来说明从税收角度来看这是否是一个重大问题。

硅谷痴迷于“信任堆叠”，国税局不喜欢它 - https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=48727963 - 2026 年 6 月

children.author: Schiendelman
children.textRawHtml: The cases you're talking about are all delaying taxation, not eliminating it. Eventually someone has to draw that wealth - the foundation has to spend for public benefit to be eligible for 501(c)3 status, for instance.
children.textZhHtml: 你说的这些案例都是推迟征税，而不是取消征税。最终必须有人提取这笔财富——例如，基金会必须为公共福利支出才有资格获得 501(c)3 资格。

children.author: toomuchtodo
children.textRawHtml:
How Elon Musk's secretive foundation hands out his billions - https://www.theguardian.com/technology/2019/jan/23/how-elon-... - January 23rd, 2019

"Spending for the public benefit" has a lot of latitude.
children.textZhHtml:
埃隆·马斯克的秘密基金会如何分发他的数十亿美元 - https://www.theguardian.com/technology/2019/jan/23/how-elon-... - 2019 年 1 月 23 日

“为公共利益支出”有很大的自由度。

children.author: saghm
children.textRawHtml: I also don't think they addressed how borrowing against the wealth doesn't require any immediate taxes (and is often low interest, given how being a billionare means you get more favorable terms). There's nothing stopping someone in that position from just deferring taxes on the money they currently have, borrowing against it, and then investing that to turn into more money with taxes deferred even further so that they can use the proceeds to pay the previous deferred taxes and keep the difference.
children.textZhHtml: 我也不认为他们谈到了如何以财富为抵押借款不需要立即缴税（而且利息通常很低，因为成为亿万富翁意味着你可以获得更优惠的条件）。没有什么可以阻止处于这种地位的人对他们目前拥有的钱进行递延纳税，以这些钱为抵押借款，然后将其投资成更多的钱，进一步递延税款，这样他们就可以用所得收益来支付之前的递延税款并保留差额。

children.author: Schiendelman
children.textRawHtml: That requires their investments to keep going up in value. That doesn't last forever, the assets that people borrow against eventually need to be sold to pay back that loan. When they sell to make payments, those are taxable events.
children.textZhHtml: 这要求他们的投资不断增值。这种情况不会永远持续下去，人们借入的资产最终需要出售来偿还贷款。当他们出售以付款时，这些都是应税事件。

children.author: saghm
children.textRawHtml: If investments didn't grow at a faster rate than interest, why would anyone ever invest money at all instead of putting into a savings account? I don't know why it''s hard to imagine that a large loan with a relatively low interest rate might be able to be invested for more than enough profits to pay off the taxes and the loan with some to spare
children.textZhHtml: 如果投资的增长速度不快于利息，为什么有人会投资而不是存入储蓄账户呢？我不知道为什么很难想象，一笔相对较低的利率的大额贷款可能能够用来投资，获得足够多的利润来偿还税款，并且还有一些剩余的贷款

children.author: Schiendelman
children.textRawHtml: Yes, and that's fine. That's still paying taxes…
children.textZhHtml: 是的，那很好。那还是要交税的……

children.author: jagged-chisel
children.textRawHtml: How does it work if the loan defaults and those assets were used as collateral?
children.textZhHtml: 如果贷款违约并且这些资产被用作抵押品，会如何运作？

children.author: Schiendelman
children.textRawHtml: I'm not a tax lawyer, but I think if you default on a loan, the collateral changes hands, which is a taxable event for you as if you sold the asset, at whatever value the unpaid portion of the loan was. So you pay tax.
children.textZhHtml: 我不是税务律师，但我认为如果您拖欠贷款，抵押品易手，这对您来说是一个应税事件，就像您出售资产一样，无论贷款中未付部分的价值是多少。所以你交税。

children.author: Schiendelman
children.textRawHtml: If any significant part of that article is true, I see self dealing that would already be against IRS code. It's just a matter of enforcement. We often already have the laws to solve the problems we identify.
children.textZhHtml: 如果该文章的任何重要部分是真实的，我认为自我交易已经违反了国税局的规定。这只是一个执行问题。我们通常已经有了解决我们发现的问题的法律。

children.author: Psillisp
children.textRawHtml: Was your ‘friend’ Jeffery Epstein?
children.textZhHtml: 你的“朋友”杰弗里·爱泼斯坦是吗？

children.author: smt88
children.textRawHtml: This report is only about wages, so even if the ultra-wealthy reported their real sources of income, they wouldn’t shut up as “labor” the way this defines it.
children.textZhHtml: 这份报告只涉及工资，因此即使超级富豪报告了他们的真实收入来源，他们也不会像本文所定义的那样以“劳工”身份闭嘴。

children.author: lotsofpulp
children.textRawHtml:
Capital gains not being considered earned income is simply sensible use of terminology to categorize different ways of amassing purchasing power. For example, in order to carry out the linked analysis.

It has nothing to do with the IRS or taxes.
children.textZhHtml:
资本利得不被视为赚取收入只是明智地使用术语来对积累购买力的不同方式进行分类。 例如，为了进行关联分析。

它与国税局或税收无关。

children.author: scottyah

children.author: saghm
children.textRawHtml:
How do you know that you're not the one hanging around the "wrong people" to know better? You could just as easily be surrounding yourself with wealthy people as they could be with non-wealthy.

Without data, it just sounds like "my social circle is more indicative of reality than yours". Maybe it is! But maybe not, so it's not particularly convincing
children.textZhHtml:
你怎么知道你不是那个和“错误的人”混在一起以了解更多信息的人？你可以很容易地与富人相处，也可以与非富人相处。

如果没有数据，听起来就像“我的社交圈比你的更能反映现实”。也许是吧！但也许不是，所以不是特别有说服力

children.author: scottyah
children.textRawHtml:
I'm not the only one with access to data though, if you're wanting to hold to your beliefs unless someone does the legwork for you and attempts to force it on you, I think your bias will overcome. Here is a source to begin anyway.

The middle class (especially upper middle) saw their share of income drop, but the bottom 50% increased.

https://equitablegrowth.org/u-s-income-data-for-2024-shows-t...
children.textZhHtml:
不过，我并不是唯一一个有权访问数据的人，如果你想坚持自己的信念，除非有人为你跑腿并试图将其强加给你，我认为你的偏见将会克服。无论如何，这是一个可以开始的来源。

中产阶级（尤其是中上阶层）的收入比例下降，但底层 50% 的人收入比例却有所增加。

https://equitablegrowth.org/u-s-income-data-for-2024-shows-t...

children.author: dfxm12
children.textRawHtml:
Talk about missing the forest for the trees. The bottom 50% saw a 0.2% increase (to just 21%) over 5 years. OK, this is technically more, but it is a paltry increase of a tiny base spread out across so many people. It is reasonably seemingly imperceivable to any individual in the group. The top 10%'s increase, on the other hand, was greater than this. A greater percentage increase on a slice of pie that was almost twice as big. In the larger context, this just shows greater inequality.

If people are saying they feel the squeeze, even in social media comments, they are probably being honest.
children.textZhHtml:
谈论只见树木不见森林。 5 年间，底层 50% 的人口增长了 0.2%（仅达到 21%）。好吧，从技术上讲，这还多，但对于分布在这么多人身上的微小基数来说，这只是微不足道的增长。对于团体中的任何个人来说，这似乎是不可察觉的。另一方面，前 10% 的增幅为 更大 比这个。一块几乎两倍大的馅饼的百分比增加更大。在更大的背景下，这只是表明 更大 不等式。

如果人们说他们感受到了压力，即使是在社交媒体评论中，他们也可能是诚实的。

children.author: MintPaw
children.textRawHtml: Data is the answer, it's just that so few people are willing to look at unbiased data. Although the start is asking a more measurable question.
children.textZhHtml: 数据就是答案，只是很少有人愿意看公正的数据。尽管一开始就提出了一个更可衡量的问题。

children.author: kudokatz
children.textRawHtml:
some maybe-biased data for a steel man in [1]:

"The Census Bureau measure overstates current income inequality between the highest and lowest 20% of earners by more than 300% and claims that income inequality has risen by 21% since 1967, when in fact it has fallen by 3% ... In 2017, among working-age households, the bottom 20% earned only $6,941 on average, and only 36% were employed. But after transfer payments and taxes, those households had an average income of $48,806. The average working-age household in the second quintile earned $31,811 and 85% of them were employed. But after transfers and taxes, they had income of $50,492, a mere 3.5% more than the bottom quintile."

[1] https://www.wsj.com/opinion/income-equality-not-inequality-i...
children.textZhHtml:
[1]中钢铁侠的一些可能有偏见的数据：

“人口普查局的衡量标准夸大了目前最高 20% 收入者和最低 20% 收入者之间的收入不平等超过 300%，并声称收入不平等自 1967 年以来上升了 21%，而事实上已经下降了 3%……2017 年，在工作年龄家庭中，最底层 20% 的人平均收入仅为 6,941 美元，只有 36% 的人有工作。但扣除转移支付和税收后，这些家庭的平均收入为第二个五分之一的工作年龄家庭平均收入为 31,811 美元，其中 85% 有工作，但扣除转移和税收后，他们的收入为 50,492 美元，仅比底部五分之一多 3.5%。

[1] https://www.wsj.com/opinion/income-equality-not-inequality-i...

children.author: roughly
children.textRawHtml: Data is also a really, really potent rhetorical tool, because it is definitionally never complete (a map that fully captures a territory is the territory), and by those omissions, the data can be made to say anything at all in a way that looks unbiased.
children.textZhHtml: 数据也是一种非常非常有效的修辞工具，因为它从定义上来说永远是不完整的（完全捕获一块领土的地图就是领土），并且通过这些遗漏，数据可以以一种看起来公正的方式表达任何内容。

children.textZhHtml: 我应该问什么问题，什么数据可以回答这个问题？我是真心实意的问

Not you particularly, I meant the original: "it feels like every share of income is at its lowest except for the ultra wealthy."

It's ambiguous in several way, no time scale, "ultra wealthy" isn't defined, and "income" somewhat ambiguous.
children.textZhHtml:
不是特别针对你，我指的是原文：“感觉除了超级富豪之外，所有收入份额都处于最低水平。”

它在很多方面都含糊不清，没有时间尺度，没有定义“超级富有”，“收入”也有些含糊。

底层 50% 的人的收入在增加，而顶层 10% 的人的收入却在增加，而过去 5 年中中产阶级的收入却在下降。这是一个快速的谷歌搜索，所以我会要求你提供一个相反的来源，否则你的评论纯粹是修辞性的并且是恶意的。

children.author: GlickWick

children.textZhHtml: 不幸的是，这个词的起源比它的含义有点黑暗。它来自希腊语中的“子宫”一词。你可以从那里填补关于它是如何产生现代意义的空白。

children.author: palmotea

We should all just stop speaking. The origin of too many words is problematic. Just think of how many were coined by racists and misogynists!
children.textZhHtml:
> 不幸的是，这个词的起源比它的含义有点黑暗。

我们都应该停止说话。太多单词的来源是有问题的。想想有多少是种族主义者和厌恶女性主义者创造的！

children.author: sapphicsnail
children.textRawHtml: Are you aware of the irony of complaining about people being hypersensitive while you're overreacting to someone explaining why some people might find a word offensive?
children.textZhHtml: 你是否意识到，当你对某人解释为什么有些人可能觉得某个词令人反感时反应过度时，却抱怨人们过于敏感，这是多么讽刺？

children.author: GlickWick
children.textRawHtml: Nah, that's knee-jerk. Don't have a particular horse in this race, just explaining why some people might react this way to the word if they're aware of the history behind it. We as a society can determine whether or not we like certain words in our vernacular.
children.textZhHtml: 不，这是下意识的。这场比赛中没有特定的马，只是解释为什么有些人如果了解这个词背后的历史，可能会对这个词做出这样的反应。作为一个社会，我们可以决定我们是否喜欢白话中的某些单词。

children.author: palmotea
children.textRawHtml: My point is: who cares about the origin of the word? IMHO, no one should. Unless the word used in a way meant to offend, it's fine. Otherwise you're kind of cultivating offense and over-sensitivity, helping it survive and grow, which helps no one.
children.textZhHtml: 我的观点是：谁关心这个词的起源？恕我直言，没有人应该这样做。除非这个词以某种方式使用 意思是 冒犯，没关系。否则你就会培养进攻性和过度敏感，帮助它生存和成长，这对任何人都没有帮助。

children.author: GlickWick
children.textRawHtml: It's trickier than that. There's a lot of people who are naturally aware of the history behind the word, and it's tough to remove emotion and intent from that history. Sometimes things bother people, and it's nice to understand why before deciding if you should do anything about it or not.
children.textZhHtml: 这比那更棘手。很多人自然而然地意识到这个词背后的历史，并且很难从这段历史中消除情感和意图。有时事情会让人烦恼，在决定是否应该采取行动之前了解原因是件好事。

children.author: tadfisher
children.textRawHtml: In particular, people with uteruses can be hyper-aware of the etymology, and I've had several inform me about it in the past, which helped me decide to avoid it.
children.textZhHtml: 特别是，有子宫的人可能对词源非常敏感，过去有好几个人告诉我这件事，这帮助我决定避免它。

children.author: GlickWick

children.textZhHtml: 不，我不在乎。只是向人们解释为什么这个定义并不是故事的全部。

comments.author: otekengineering
comments.textRawHtml:
isn't this the expected/intended outcome of the economic policy we've held for a generation or two?

the capital/labor class gap increases when total returns on capital investment exceed total wages+redistribution to the labor class, and the gap shrinks when that's reversed. the market ~controls the capital gains and labor wages knobs, and society decides where to set the redistribution knob.

the article investigating the post-covid drop and concluding that it's normal is an interesting rhetorical device. on one hand, relief that nothing crazy is happening. on the other hand, disappointment that we've accepted a growing inequality gap as normal. the gap was already at a post-war max going into covid, the floor gave out 20 years earlier and covid was just gas on the fire.

advancements in automation and tax codes that benefit capex over payroll will continue to incentivize business to shift budgets from labor to robots.
comments.textZhHtml:
这不是我们一两代人所采取的经济政策的预期/预期结果吗？

当资本投资总回报超过总工资+对劳动阶级的再分配时，资本/劳动阶级差距就会扩大，而当情况相反时，差距就会缩小。 市场控制资本收益和劳动工资旋钮，社会决定在哪里设置再分配旋钮。

这篇文章调查了新冠疫情后的下降并得出结论认为这是正常的，这是一个有趣的修辞手法。 一方面，因为没有发生任何疯狂的事情而松了口气。 另一方面，我们对不平等差距扩大视为正常现象感到失望。 进入新冠疫情期间，差距已经达到了战后的最大值，地板早在 20 年前就已经损坏，而新冠疫情只是火上浇油。

自动化和税法的进步有利于资本支出而非工资，将继续激励企业将预算从劳动力转向机器人。

children.author: hn_throwaway_99
children.textRawHtml:
Thanks very much for providing those sources.

That said, it's a huge pet peeve of mine when someone makes a statement, and then provides sources to back up that statement, but the actual sources contradict their original statement.

You stated "but AIUI this is mostly a statistical illusion caused by changes to US tax law- previously income that was attributed to 'labor' shifted over to LLCs/S corps for more beneficial tax rates." But then your very first linked article states "First, about a third of the decline in the published labor share appears to be an artifact of statistical procedures used to impute the labor income of the self-employed that underlies the headline measure."

I think there is a huge difference between 1/3 (while still a lot and an important factor) and what you wrote, "mostly a statistical illusion", especially since other substantial factors proposed in that article are things like offshoring.
children.textZhHtml:
非常感谢您提供这些来源。

也就是说，当有人发表声明，然后提供支持该声明的消息来源时，我非常恼火， 但实际来源与他们最初的说法相矛盾。

你说“但这主要是美国税法变化造成的统计错觉——以前归因于‘劳动力’的收入转移到有限责任公司/小型企业以获得更优惠的税率。”但你的第一篇链接文章指出“首先，已公布的劳动份额下降的大约三分之一似乎是统计程序的产物，用于估算作为标题指标基础的个体经营者的劳动收入。”

我认为 1/3 （虽然仍然很多而且是一个重要因素）和你写的“主要是统计错觉”之间存在巨大差异， 尤其 因为该文章中提出的其他重要因素是离岸外包等。

children.author: hash872
children.textRawHtml: I'm a bit skeptical that you absorbed these 3 pieces in the time it took you to respond (they are 27, 29, and 63 pages, respectively), but they find a range of effect sizes here- the other two a bit more strongly. I feel comfortable with my original statement, though I suppose there's always room for nitpicking
children.textZhHtml: 我有点怀疑你在做出回应的时间内吸收了这 3 篇文章（它们分别是 27、29 和 63 页），但他们在这里发现了一系列的效果大小 - 其他两篇更强烈一些。我对我最初的陈述感到满意，尽管我认为总有吹毛求疵的余地

children.author: hn_throwaway_99
children.textRawHtml:
No, I completely read the first article. It was quite clear in what percentage of the decline in labor's share that it attributed to differences in statistical assessment of sole proprietors. I then looked at the abstracts of the other two. The third one basically completely agreed with the first ("We find that reallocating activity to the form it would have taken prior to the Tax Reform Act of 1986 accounts for 30% of the decline in the corporate sector labor share between 1978 and 2017."), and that's when I wrote my reply.

Frankly, I don't know how you can call this "nitpicking". In no definition in the English language is "one third" remotely the same as "mostly".
children.textZhHtml:
不，我完整地阅读了第一篇文章。很明显，劳动力份额下降的百分比是由于个体经营者统计评估的差异造成的。然后我看了另外两个的摘要。第三个基本上完全同意第一个（“我们发现，1978 年至 2017 年间企业部门劳动力份额下降 30% 的原因是，将活动重新分配到 1986 年税收改革法案之前的形式。”），这就是我写下回复的时候。

坦白说，我不知道你怎么能称之为“吹毛求疵”。在英语中，“三分之一”的定义与“大多数”完全不同。

children.author: win311fwg
children.textRawHtml:
Not much. Early economies relied on mutual trade. I give you X and in return you give me Y. Soon we realized that you cannot always give Y in return immediately, so we invented accounting to keep track of your promise to give me Y at some point in the future. As time went by eventually we stopped caring about getting Y back in return and started taking an interest in collecting the promises themselves (i.e. profit).

Why would someone want to collect promises? That seems rather silly, right? What having a lot of promises gives you is social standing. People treat you differently — better — when they give you their promises. If traditional labor goes away, the economy simply becomes you promising to hold those who have things in the highest regard; to be there as their friend when they call for you to. That is the same modern economy we already have but with less steps.
children.textZhHtml:
不多。早期经济依赖相互贸易。我给你 X，作为回报，你给我 Y。很快我们意识到你不能总是立即给予 Y 作为回报，因此我们发明了会计来跟踪你在未来某个时候给我 Y 的承诺。随着时间的推移，最终我们不再关心 Y 的回报，而是开始对收取承诺本身（即利润）感兴趣。

为什么有人想要收集承诺？这看起来很愚蠢，对吧？拥有很多承诺可以给你带来社会地位。当人们向你做出承诺时，他们会以不同的方式对待你，甚至更好。如果传统的劳动消失了，经济就变成了你承诺给予那些拥有东西的人以最高的尊重；当他们呼唤你时，作为他们的朋友在那里。这与我们已经拥有的现代经济相同，但步骤更少。

children.author: ux266478
children.textRawHtml:
I think it's more nuanced than that. There surely are plenty of cases where that is the case, but it's also a natural effect of hyperfinancialization, which many really do believe to be a "net positive" for the stability it brings. There's also the natural tendency of consolidation and centralization of power, and the natural counter-balance to that has been suppressed. Then you have legislative incompetence, the general failings of scientific governance aggregating over time, and many other structural flaws that are deeply seated and long-running.

We shouldn't just be pointing at the (very much real) stupid greed, there are many rotten components occurring simultaneously.
children.textZhHtml:
我认为这比这更微妙。确实有很多例子都是这种情况，但这也是超级金融化的自然效应，许多人确实相信这对其带来的稳定性具有“净积极作用”。还有权力巩固和集中的自然趋势，而对此的自然平衡却被抑制了。然后是立法无能、科学治理的普遍失败随着时间的推移而累积，以及许多其他根深蒂固且长期存在的结构性缺陷。

我们不应该仅仅指出（非常真实的）愚蠢的贪婪，有许多腐烂的成分同时发生。

comments.textRawHtml: Because labors in China are extremely cheap. And they are getting cheaper and cheaper in last decade, despite the GDP growth.
comments.textZhHtml: 因为中国的劳动力极其廉价。尽管国内生产总值在增长，但在过去十年中它们的价格却越来越便宜。

children.author: seizethecheese
children.textRawHtml:
As a hardware founder, I’ve seen the opposite until very recently. Labor cost has been going up much faster than inflation, at lease in hardware assembly.

Do you have a source?
children.textZhHtml:
作为一名硬件创始人，直到最近我才看到相反的情况。劳动力成本的上涨速度远远快于通货膨胀，至少在硬件组装方面是这样。

你有消息来源吗？

comments.author: lenerdenator
comments.textRawHtml:
Equity should be a normal part of compensation for non-executive employees in addition to their wage/salary and benefits.

There's nothing about being in the C-suite that magically endows one with motivation based upon stock price, but we pretend that there is.
comments.textZhHtml:
除工资和福利外，股权应成为非执行员工薪酬的正常组成部分。

担任最高管理层并不能神奇地赋予一个人基于股价的动力，但我们假装有。

comments.author: macinjosh
comments.textRawHtml: The longer I participate in the economy the more it starts to feel like the end stages of a game of monopoly. There is nothing left to own unless you already are wealthy and the only way to get ahead is luck.
comments.textZhHtml: 我参与经济的时间越长，就越感觉像是垄断游戏的最后阶段。除非你已经很富有，否则你就没有什么可以拥有的了，而成功的唯一途径就是运气。

children.author: luizfzs
children.textRawHtml: The 2 main differences between our economy and Monopoly is that 1) in ours, some people start with properties and more money in hand and 2) a Monopoly game takes less time to end.
children.textZhHtml: 我们的经济和大富翁经济之间的两个主要区别是：1）在我们的经济中，有些人从财产和手头更多的钱开始，2）大富翁游戏需要更少的时间结束。

comments.author: gcanyon
comments.textRawHtml: Is there a reason to think this is anything more than increased productivity? And that therefore the case has to be made that labor, more than capital, should benefit from that increase? I’m not saying that argument can’t or shouldn’t be made, just saying that “it was different before!” Isn’t the best argument.
comments.textZhHtml: 有理由认为这不仅仅是提高生产力吗？因此，劳动力（而不是资本）应该从这种增长中受益？我并不是说不能或不应该争论，只是说“以前不一样！”不是最好的论据。

comments.author: ghastmaster
comments.textRawHtml:
The most interesting takeaway I see in the labor share percentage graph is the trend that labor share increases into the recession. Post recession share trends down for a bit.

How much of the trend is due to employment trends vs. printing money for the wealthy to get their hands on it first(and profit) post recession?
comments.textZhHtml:
我在劳动力份额百分比图表中看到的最有趣的结论是，劳动力份额在经济衰退期间呈上升趋势。经济衰退后的股价趋势略有下降。

这一趋势有多少是由于就业趋势与为富人印钞以在经济衰退后首先获得（和利润）造成的？

comments.author: mrtksn
comments.textRawHtml:
And yet for some reason all the algorithmic pricing targets the laborers, when apps hunt for whales they target teenagers. Ridiculous.

There is a need for proper pricing for the rich, i.e. Elon can pay a million dollars per meal. Someone is leaving money on the table.
comments.textZhHtml:
然而出于某种原因，所有的算法定价都以劳动者为目标，而当应用程序寻找鲸鱼时，它们的目标是青少年。荒谬的。

富人需要适当的定价，即埃隆每顿饭可以支付一百万美元。有人把钱留在桌子上。

children.author: Kbelicius
children.textRawHtml:
Ha, you just reminded me of something that I read a long time ago. Some history book which relayed a story from some part of islamic world from centuries ago.

A king goes hunting but fails to catch anything. Hungry, he goes to a nearby village. He enters the inn and orders some quail eggs. After finishing the meal, he goes to pay. The innkeeper tells him that the meal costs ten gold coins.

"I did not know quail eggs were so rare in these parts," says the king.

"They are not," replies the innkeeper, "but kings are."
children.textZhHtml:
哈，你让我想起了很久以前读过的东西。一些历史书讲述了几个世纪前伊斯兰世界某些地区的故事。

国王去打猎，但一无所获。饿了，他去了附近的一个村庄。他走进旅馆，点了一些鹌鹑蛋。吃完饭，他去结账。店主告诉他，一顿饭要十个金币。

“我不知道鹌鹑蛋在这些地方如此稀有，”国王说。

“他们不是，”旅店老板回答道，“但国王是。”

children.author: playorizaya
children.textRawHtml:
Love this.

Parking and speeding tickets should have income brackets, at least.

In the early Internet I saw this thing, no idea if it’s true but it sounds good (someone can math check it), goes something like:

A person pays $2 to play basketball on a public court.

Michael Jordan gets paid $2k to play on the same one.

A person pays $100 for basketball shoes.

Michael Jordan gets paid $100k to wear the same ones.

A person pays $40 to go see a basketball game.

Jordan gets paid $400k to attend the same game.

Michael Jordan makes about $5 per second.

If Michael Jordan saved all his money without spending a penny for 250 years…

He wouldn’t even have half as much as Bill Gates!

It made me think differently about money and consumer spending.
children.textZhHtml:
喜欢这个。

停车罚单和超速罚单至少应该有收入等级。

在早期的互联网上，我看到了这个东西，不知道它是不是真的，但听起来不错（有人可以数学检查它），大概是这样的：

一个人支付 2 美元在公共球场打篮球。

迈克尔·乔丹（Michael Jordan）在同一场比赛中获得了 2000 美元的报酬。

一个人花 100 美元购买篮球鞋。

迈克尔乔丹穿着同样的衣服可以获得 10 万美元的报酬。

一个人花 40 美元去看一场篮球比赛。

乔丹观看同一场比赛的报酬是 40 万美元。

迈克尔·乔丹每秒大约赚 5 美元。

如果迈克尔·乔丹 250 年不花一分钱就把所有的钱都存起来了……

他的财产连比尔盖茨的一半都不到！

这让我对金钱和消费者支出有了不同的看法。

children.author: mrtksn
children.textRawHtml:
> Parking and speeding tickets should have income brackets, at least

AFAIK in some countries this exist but my case is more capitalist oriented. Rich people obviously can pay more since they keep accumulating wealth. It is an obvious sub optimal pricing since the low and even middle class rent/mortgage and other services quickly approaching the most they can pay so they can’t actually save and make wealth.
children.textZhHtml:
> 停车罚单和超速罚单至少应该有收入等级

据我所知，在某些国家存在这种情况，但我的情况更倾向于资本主义。富人显然可以支付更多，因为他们不断积累财富。这是一个明显的次优定价，因为低收入甚至中产阶级的租金/抵押贷款和其他服务很快就接近他们可以支付的最高限额，因此他们实际上无法储蓄和创造财富。

children.author: playorizaya
children.textRawHtml:
sub optimal for who? ;)

A long time ago I was working for a mid tier founder up in Oregon.

Pipe dream of a startup, no customers, no product. Money and vision, but no direction and the main problem was that nobody would ever tell him "No" (me included) - to everyone's great detriment.

He would certainly fail at this endeavor...

He was the main investor, after all.

At some point we brought on web marketing help - he knew people at Nike. To my surprise, they actually partnered with us - Nike! He also co-owned a web design agency in Portland, so I started working with designers and marketers there.

When I would travel there, he always set me up in the same hotel. Turns out (a year later), he owned it. He owned the damn laundromat next door, too, because it was adjacent to the apartment complex he owned.

After literally 1 beer, we were now going to do a live, in person event in Portland - he knew people and could make it happen. He owned the building where the event would take place.

At the event he started asking if I'd ever leave San Francisco, if I liked Portland. We started talking about rent and stuff.

I find out he's a landlord there - owns several single-family homes (with awful cyclone fencing) and has tenants around Portland. He starts telling me prices etc. Asks me what people are paying in SF. We both laugh over how expensive SF is - sun shafts illuminating our amber beer glasses as we drink to... Idk what.

What happened was I found out that he was rich as fuck, and owned the god damn town, and everything else. His startup was a joke and failed - but who cares, it was a frame in a movie. I cared about it more than he did.

Sub optimal for who??
children.textZhHtml:
对谁来说次优？ ;)

很久以前，我在俄勒冈州为一位中层创始人工作。

初创公司的白日梦，没有客户，就没有产品。有金钱和远见，但没有方向，主要问题是没有人会告诉他“不”（包括我）——这对每个人都造成了极大的损害。

他这次的努力肯定会失败……

毕竟他是主要投资者。

在某个时候，我们带来了网络营销帮助——他认识耐克的人。让我惊讶的是，他们居然和我们合作——耐克！他还在波特兰与他人共同拥有一家网页设计机构，所以我开始与那里的设计师和营销人员合作。

当我去那里旅行时，他总是把我安排在同一家酒店。事实证明（一年后），他拥有了它。他还拥有隔壁那家该死的自助洗衣店，因为它毗邻他拥有的公寓大楼。

喝完一瓶啤酒后，我们现在要在波特兰举办一场现场的、面对面的活动——他认识人们并且可以实现这一目标。他拥有举办活动的大楼。

在活动中，他开始问我是否会离开旧金山，是否喜欢波特兰。我们开始谈论租金之类的事情。

我发现他是那里的房东 - 拥有几套单户住宅（带有糟糕的旋风围栏），并且在波特兰周围有租户。他开始告诉我价格等。问我人们在旧金山付的钱是多少。我们都嘲笑旧金山的昂贵——当我们喝酒时，太阳轴照亮了我们的琥珀色啤酒杯……我不知道什么。

发生的事情是我发现他他妈的很有钱，拥有这个该死的小镇，还有其他一切。他的创业是一个笑话并且失败了——但谁在乎呢，这只是电影中的一个画面。我比他更关心这个。

对谁来说次优？

comments.author: chasing
comments.textRawHtml: By design, no?
comments.textZhHtml: 设计使然，不是吗？

children.author: torginus
children.textRawHtml: Yes by design. Basically just by designating one part of the economy as non-productive consumption economy, and the other part as production economy, they create the justification for inflation - so that the government can have free money and take it from the stupid consumers by decreasing their wealth a little every year should they choose not to spend it, and give it (sorry, invest) to the nice productive entrepreneurial capitalist part of enconomy who will somehow reinvest it allegedly (read: mostly buy up shit the former half needs and sell it back to them at markup), the system has a direct wealth transfer mechanism that points from the poor to the rich, it couldn't be any more shameless and obvious if they tried.
children.textZhHtml: 是的，设计使然。基本上只是通过将经济的一部分指定为非生产性消费经济，而另一部分指定为生产经济，他们就为通货膨胀创造了理由——这样政府就可以拥有免费的钱，并从愚蠢的人那里拿走它 消费者 通过每年一点点减少他们的财富，如果他们选择不花它，并将其（对不起，投资）给那些有生产力的人 创业型的 经济的资本主义部分据称会以某种方式对其进行再投资（读：主要是购买前半部分需要的粪便，然后以加价卖回给他们），该系统有一个直接的财富转移机制，从穷人到富人，如果他们尝试的话，那就再无耻和明显了。

comments.author: ETH_start
comments.textRawHtml: I wonder if labor itself will become an anachronism in the age of AI. Perhaps the future economic landscape will be dominated by capital because everyone will own capital. You will command a small army of agents to do whatever you want. You will no longer need to work for someone. You own small businesses far more than you could possibly operate in the pre-AI era and they will mostly operate autonomously with minimal direction and some guidance from you.
comments.textZhHtml: 我想知道在人工智能时代，劳动本身是否会变得不合时宜。也许未来的经济格局将由资本主导，因为每个人都将拥有资本。你将指挥一小支特工军队做任何你想做的事。您将不再需要为某人工作。你拥有的小企业远远超过你在前人工智能时代所能经营的规模，它们大多会在你的最少指导和一些指导下自主运营。

children.author: lesam
children.textRawHtml:
Assuming your labour contribution to these agents is 'minimal':

Why would you own them, instead of some well capitalized billionaire?

To the extent that you do have capital, why do you assume that your 'minimal direction and guidance' would outcompete a full time specialist working for that billionaire?
children.textZhHtml:
假设您对这些代理的劳动贡献“最小”：

为什么你会拥有它们，而不是某个资本雄厚的亿万富翁？

To the extent that you do have capital, why do you assume that your 'minimal direction and guidance' would outcompete a full time specialist working for that billionaire?

children.author: ETH_start
children.textRawHtml:
Assuming agent costs keep declining exponentially as they have over the last three years, why would everyone not own some agents? It's not like the number of agents is capped and the billionaires hoard all of them. I imagine it would be more like smartphones, where there were only 50 million smartphones after the iPhone was first released in 2007 and now there are something like three billion. They become more accessible and plentiful over time. Same thing should apply to agents.

And in this world of abundant agents, what advantage does the billionaire have exactly over the non-billionaire? Their employees are less motivated than owner-operators, and they no longer have the scale advantage that large corporations used to have. Each individual can effectively operate like a large corporation, because each individual can have their own large synthetic workforce at very low cost. The scarce resource here then becomes uniquely human insights and real motivation, which entrepreneurs are always going to have more of than employees.
children.textZhHtml:
假设代理成本像过去三年一样持续呈指数下降，为什么每个人都不会拥有一些代理呢？代理人的数量并不是有上限的，而是亿万富翁们把他们全部囤积起来。我想它更像是智能手机，2007 年 iPhone 首次发布后，智能手机只有 5000 万部，而现在大约有 30 亿部。随着时间的推移，它们变得更加容易获得和丰富。同样的事情也应该适用于代理人。

在这个代理人众多的世界里，亿万富翁相对于非亿万富翁到底有什么优势？他们的员工的积极性不如业主经营者，而且他们不再拥有大公司曾经拥有的规模优势。每个人都可以像大公司一样有效地运作，因为每个人都可以以非常低的成本拥有自己的大型合成劳动力。这里的稀缺资源就变成了人类独特的洞察力和真正的动力，企业家总是比员工拥有更多的这些资源。

children.author: stephenhuey
children.textRawHtml: I'm hoping it's likely the powerful cannot monopolize agents. If open models are easy to obtain, and every homestead has solar and large batteries, everyone can routinely charge their robots like Seven of Nine plugging into her Borg alcove. As long as they have the right to repair them, and a monopoly can't fleece you with robot maintenance!
children.textZhHtml: 我希望强者不能垄断代理商。如果开放模型很容易获得，并且每个家园都有太阳能和大型电池，那么每个人都可以定期为他们的机器人充电，就像九之七插入她的博格壁龛一样。只要他们有维修权，垄断者就不能用机器人维修来敲诈你！

children.author: logicchains
children.textRawHtml: It's basic economics; larger firms become progressively less efficient  for the same reason that communist command economies are inefficient, because there's no internal price signals to guide resource allocation. So there's a natural cap on how big a firm can get (in information theoretic terms, there's a hard limit on the amount of information a centralized structure can process effectively).
children.textZhHtml: 这是基本的经济学；大公司的效率逐渐降低，其原因与共产主义指令经济效率低下的原因相同，因为没有内部价格信号来指导资源配置。因此，公司的规模有一个自然的上限（用信息论的术语来说，集中式结构可以有效处理的信息量存在硬性限制）。

children.author: torginus
children.textRawHtml:
We live in a world with a severe housing crisis - not shortage, as there are usually enough units, people just can't afford them.

One would assume the difficulty of building housing has gone down with the general progress of technology - and if all else fails, you can just do what they did 50, 100 years ago where affordability was far less a struggle - people, who had less income in real terms spent proportionally less on it.

So did society devolve that an unit of industrial output has become more expensive? Or did money and resources just go into a parallel 'rich people economy', that has created a constant drain on the resources of average people?
children.textZhHtml:
We live in a world with a severe housing crisis - not shortage, as there are usually enough units, people just can't afford them.

人们会认为，随着技术的普遍进步，建造住房的难度已经下降——如果其他一切都失败了，你可以像他们 50 或 100 年前所做的那样，那时负担能力远没有那么困难——实际收入较低的人们在住房上的支出比例也相应减少。

So did society devolve that an unit of industrial output has become more expensive? Or did money and resources just go into a parallel 'rich people economy', that has created a constant drain on the resources of average people?

children.author: ETH_start
children.textRawHtml:
Housing is exceptionally vulnerable to capture because it is immobile. AI on the otherhand should see its cost continue to plummet due to competition. Per token costs have already fallen exponentially over the last three years.

I'm not sure if this addresses the point you're trying to make though. If not, please clarify your argument for me.
children.textZhHtml:
住房非常容易被占领，因为它是不可移动的。另一方面，由于竞争，人工智能的成本应该会继续下降。在过去三年里，每个代币的成本已经呈指数级下降。

我不确定这是否解决了您想要表达的观点。如果没有，请为我澄清你的论点。

children.author: globular-toast
children.textRawHtml: Until you can eat an agent it's all worthless.
children.textZhHtml: 在你能吃掉药剂之前，一切都是毫无价值的。

comments.author: fsckboy
comments.textRawHtml:
there is no such thing as "labor share of income". It's a measurement you can calculate, but it means nothing tells nothing.

in caveman times, labor's share of income was 100%. Do you want to live like that?
comments.textZhHtml:
不存在“收入中的劳动份额”这样的说法。这是一个可以计算的测量值，但这意味着什么都不能说明什么。

在穴居人时代，劳动收入占收入的100%。你想这样生活吗？

comments.author: shadow_it
comments.textRawHtml: right, so now more than ever... if you're not saving/investing you are falling behind.
comments.textZhHtml: 是的，所以现在比以往任何时候都更……如果你不储蓄/投资，你就会落后。

comments.author: nimbius
comments.textRawHtml:
this is relatively unremarkable for those with an understanding of wage, labor, profit, price and capital.

capitalism will always seek to reduce labor cost. during the epoch of neoliberalism it achieved great strides in this by reducing labor power through union busting by both thatcher and reagan in the UK and US respectively. it has also effectively curtailed any increase in the minimum wage for nearly 20 years as well as reduced protections, regulation and prosecution for wage theft and overtime pay violations which it maintains as exclusively as civil matters while ensuring theft itself from a merchant in turn is always a criminal matter through the primacy of private property.

to learn more i recommend reading Marx's "Das Kapital," albeit its rather academic. Engels "wage labor" is also a good read to understand why housing is so persistently unaffortable but helps to understand why any other good or service slowly becomes so as well.
comments.textZhHtml:
对于那些了解工资、劳动力、利润、价格和资本的人来说，这相对来说是不寻常的。

资本主义总是寻求降低劳动力成本。 在新自由主义时代，英国和美国的撒切尔和里根分别通过破坏工会来减少劳动力，从而在这方面取得了长足的进步。 它还有效地限制了近20年来最低工资的增长，并减少了对工资盗窃和加班费违法行为的保护、监管和起诉，它仅将其视为民事问题，同时通过私有财产的首要地位确保对商人的盗窃本身始终是刑事问题。

要了解更多信息，我建议阅读马克思的《资本论》，尽管它相当学术。 恩格斯的《雇佣劳动》也是一本很好的读物，它可以帮助我们理解为什么住房一直难以负担，但也有助于理解为什么其他商品或服务也会慢慢变得如此难以负担。

children.author: torginus
children.textRawHtml:
The problem is we are one step beyond capitalist exploitation as described by Marx - basically the surplus which fatcat industrialists extract from the laborer does not really exist - you have to compete in the market with others who do the same, and offer things at the lowest possible margins, and if you need to be big enough to get capital in, you have investors who demand their profits.

So basically you are squeezed between the public demanding lower prices and the investors demanding record returns. If you are not a monopoly, that is an impossible ask

Basically the only truly profitable businesses left out there is selling hopes and dreams to investors, and shovels to those who build them, which just about describes tech & AI, with companies who regularly manage to 10x their valuations (and P/E ratios)
children.textZhHtml:
问题是，我们已经超越了马克思所描述的资本主义剥削——基本上，肥大的实业家从劳动者那里榨取的剩余并不真正存在——你必须在市场上与其他做同样事情的人竞争，并以尽可能低的利润提供产品，如果你需要足够大才能获得资本，你就会有要求利润的投资者。

因此，基本上你被夹在要求更低价格的公众和要求创纪录回报的投资者之间。如果你不是垄断者，这是一个不可能的要求

基本上，唯一真正盈利的业务就是向投资者出售希望和梦想，并向建造它们的人出售铲子，这恰好描述了科技和人工智能，而这些公司的估值（和市盈率）经常达到 10 倍

children.author: aeternum
children.textRawHtml:
Marx fails to imagine a world in which labor actually has little to no value.

His worldview is primarily that capitalists 'steal' the valuable labor. However it doesn't seem that that is actually the world we are in. Instead the intrinsic value of human labor seems to be slowly trending towards zero.

And it kind of makes sense, same has happened with oxen labor, horse labor, etc.
children.textZhHtml:
马克思未能想象出一个劳动实际上几乎没有价值的世界。

他的世界观主要是资本家“窃取”了宝贵的劳动力。 然而，这似乎并不是我们真正所处的世界。相反，人类劳动的内在价值似乎正在慢慢趋向于零。

这是有道理的，同样的情况也发生在牛的劳动、马的劳动等方面。

children.author: saghm
children.textRawHtml:
> And it kind of makes sense, same has happened with oxen labor, horse labor, etc.

Sounds like we should start imagining a world where we don't treat people like literal livestock, and then figure out how to get there fast
children.textZhHtml:
> 这是有道理的，同样的情况也发生在牛劳动、马劳动等方面。

听起来我们应该开始想象一个我们不把人当作牲畜对待的世界，然后找出如何快速实现这一目标

children.author: wqaatwt
children.textRawHtml: Isn’t it the complete opposite? i.e. high automatization means that a single worker can create many times more value than before. However it reduces the demand for labor and worker bargaining power. So companies have no incentive to pair “fair” wages.
children.textZhHtml: 这不是完全相反吗？即高度自动化意味着单个工人可以创造比以前多很多倍的价值。然而，它降低了对劳动力的需求和工人的议价能力。因此，公司没有动力提供“公平”的工资。

children.author: aeternum
children.textRawHtml:
That is true but likely only temporarily. Why is the single worker even needed?

We tend to have a pretty human-centric worldview so if there's a single human working to keep a hotel running, our default is to attribute all the generated value to them when it really isn't the case. You can imagine that hotel at some point in the near future goes from requiring 1 worker to keep it running to zero.
children.textZhHtml:
这是事实，但可能只是暂时的。 为什么还需要单身工人？

我们倾向于拥有以人为本的世界观，因此，如果只有一个人努力维持酒店的运营，我们的默认设置是将所有生成的价值都归因于他们，而事实并非如此。 您可以想象，在不久的将来的某个时候，酒店将不再需要 1 名员工来保持其运转，而是减少到零。

children.author: wqaatwt
children.textRawHtml:
> is the single worker even needed

Because he is? I do not expect for some sort of universal “AGI” to emerge within my lifetime which would supplant humans in every area (of course what do I know.. but still there is no real indication we are even remotely close to that currently)

> default is to attribute all the generated value to them when it really isn't the case

Certainly, you should attribute a significant proportion to the people who installed the automated system and even more to those who designed it.
children.textZhHtml:
> 是否需要单个工人

因为他是？我不期望在我的有生之年出现某种通用的“AGI”，它会在各个领域取代人类（当然我知道什么……但仍然没有真正的迹象表明我们目前距离这一目标还很遥远）

> 默认情况下，当实际情况并非如此时，将所有生成的值归因于它们

当然，你应该将很大一部分归功于安装自动化系统的人，甚至更多地归功于设计它的人。

children.author: aeternum
children.textRawHtml:
>Certainly, you should attribute a significant proportion to the people who installed the automated system and even more to those who designed it.

Indeed, but what happens if/when they choose to sell the system and someone else buys it? Now the owner is who is generating the value. Even if he hires some maintenance worker to come by once and awhile and check up on it, it is the system (aka the capital), not the worker that is generating the vast majority of the value.
children.textZhHtml:
>当然，你应该将很大一部分归功于安装自动化系统的人，甚至更多地归功于设计它的人。

确实如此，但是如果/当他们选择出售系统而其他人购买它时会发生什么？ 现在，所有者就是创造价值的人。 即使他雇佣一些维护人员偶尔过来检查一下，也是系统（又名资本）而不是工人创造了绝大多数价值。

If you landscaper had one like the plumbers do he'd have his own yacht.

Or he wouldn't exist because you'd buy about as much of his services as you do a plumber's.

如果园艺师像水管工一样拥有一艘游艇，他就会拥有自己的游艇。

否则他就不会存在，因为你购买他的服务与购买水管工的服务一样多。

children.author: anthonypasq
children.textRawHtml: low skilled human labor is going to zero. high skill approaches infinity.
children.textZhHtml: 低技能劳动力将趋于零。高技能接近无穷大。

children.author: torginus
children.textRawHtml:
> Marx fails to imagine a world in which labor actually has little to no value.

Marxism was an idea formulated especially as a reaction against a world where labor has lost almost all of its value. Which is precisely the origin of capitalism - the idea that money itself can be productive, and thus people who have lots of money can be expected to get more of it.

This was an untrue idea for most of human history, outside of the circles of moneylending and banking.
children.textZhHtml:
> 马克思未能想象出一个劳动实际上几乎没有价值的世界。

马克思主义是一种专门针对劳动力几乎失去所有价值的世界而制定的思想。这正是资本主义的起源——金钱本身可以产生生产力，因此拥有大量金钱的人可以期望得到更多的金钱。

对于人类历史上的大部分时间来说，除了放贷和银行业之外，这都是一个不真实的想法。

children.author: AlotOfReading
children.textRawHtml:
It doesn't seem like the value of human labor is going away. If you look at luxury goods, they're still "handmade". Telecoms still advertise human representatives. Nursing homes still charge massive amounts for personal service.

What's changing is how much of that surplus value is captured by the workers doing the labor.
children.textZhHtml:
人类劳动的价值似乎并没有消失。如果你看看奢侈品，它们仍然是“手工制作”的。电信公司仍在为人类代表做广告。疗养院仍然收取大量的个人服务费用。

正在发生变化的是，有多少剩余价值被从事劳动的工人攫取了。

children.author: abdullahkhalids
children.textRawHtml:
> Workers enjoy highest living standards of any time in history.

It's entirely possible for someone to be paid a lot in absolute terms, while at the same time paid very little relative to the value that they produce which is monetarily captured by their organization. The truth of the first does not invalidate the injustice of the second.
children.textZhHtml:
> 工人们享有历史上最高的生活水平。

某人完全有可能获得很高的绝对报酬，而同时相对于他们所创造的价值（被组织以货币形式获取）而言，所得到的报酬却很少。第一个事实的真相并不能证明第二个事实的不公正。

comments.author: kevmo
comments.textRawHtml:
This is clearly heading in a direction where the USA is going to elect a huge number of socialists, who in turn are going to enact massive taxes on billionaires and break up the monopolies.[1]

This is why I think the billionaire oligarchs are literally mentally ill. They've won the entire game. They control everything. They live like gods, they twitch a pinky and millions dance.

But their response to all of this power is to seek even more of it, destabilizing the very system that has them on top. You would think self-preservation would kick in. The fact that it is not and that their greed knows apparently no bounds is going to lead to their extinction.

comments.author: jmyeet
comments.textRawHtml:
Now consider this against the rising productivity-pay gap that has been widening since teh 1970s [1].

The big picture here is increasing wealth inequality and that has been on steroids since the pandemic.

The only shocking part to me is how people continually and intentionally don't see it or, worse, think they'll be unaffected by it so don't care. You see this on HN where so many people seem to think they'll be Jeff Bezos one day.

But even if that's true, don't you want to live in a society where you don't need armed guards at your house and you don't need armed escorts to go anywhere? Because that's what we're heading towards. One of the problems with American society (in particular) being so car-centric is that it lets people insulate themselves from the rest of society more easily. In cities like NYC you're forced to see and deal with the less fortunate. You can't hide from it so easily.

We don't need trillionaires. We need to raise basic living standards so people have food and shelter and we don't need to separate society into slums and armored compounds.

[1]: https://www.epi.org/productivity-pay-gap/
comments.textZhHtml:
现在考虑一下自 20 世纪 70 年代以来不断扩大的生产率与薪酬差距的情况[1]。

这里的大局是财富不平等的加剧，而且自大流行以来这种不平等一直在加剧。

对我来说唯一令人震惊的是人们如何不断地、故意地看不到它，或者更糟糕的是，认为他们不会受到它的影响，所以不在乎。你可以在 HN 上看到这一点，那里很多人似乎都认为他们有一天会成为杰夫·贝佐斯。

但即使这是真的，难道你不想生活在一个家里不需要武装警卫、去任何地方都不需要武装护卫的社会吗？因为这就是我们正在努力的方向。美国社会（尤其是）如此以汽车为中心的问题之一是，它让人们更容易将自己与社会其他部分隔离开来。在纽约这样的城市，你被迫去见识并与那些不幸的人打交道。你不能这么轻易地躲避它。

我们不需要万亿富翁。我们需要提高基本生活水平，以便人们有食物和住所，我们不需要将社会分成贫民窟和装甲院。

[1]： https://www.epi.org/productivity-pay-gap/
